mnemonics: Direct, Set, Associative: Map Your Cache Addresses (DSA-MYCA) for the three mapping functions.
keyPoints: Direct Mapping: Each main memory block maps to exactly one specific cache line, calculated by (Block Address) MOD (Number of Cache Lines).
Set-Associative Mapping: A compromise where the cache is divided into sets; each set contains 'n' lines, and a memory block maps to any line within a specific set.
Fully Associative Mapping: A memory block can be placed in any line of the cache, offering the most flexibility but requiring complex hardware for searching.
commonMistakes: Confusing block offset bits with index bits; the offset selects the byte/word within a block, while the index selects the cache line/set.
Assuming a larger cache size always decreases the miss rate; the mapping function and access patterns (like conflicts in direct mapping) are equally critical.
